sentences.text: Hard?
Very difficult time.
In Monowitz-- well, there were a lot of Polish, you call it, Polish prisoners there, and now and again one escaped.
And-- because they speak the language.
You see, we couldn't escape.
If we could escape-- it always ways to escape.
You can hide yourself, or in a car or a transport, or foot.
There always now and again an escaped one.
And the Germans, they didn't have it.
If one escaped, they took reciprocal against as many as they want to.
And they picked out-- and I was very lucky-- and they picked out 20 boys, and I was one of the 20.
Just picked out at random, 20 boys.
And we had to stay 20-- I stand 24 hours between two barbed wire fences.
But how you gonna stand 24 hours?
How wide was the--
Not wide.
Maybe two meters, one and a half meter.
You know, they always-- everything, we always doubled.
And one was under-- how do you call it?
Electric, sorry-- I call it electric-- electrocuted, yeah?
And you see them falling against it, and you were gone.
Used to do that, and let them hang.
They just let them hang.
They were cruel.
They-- they-- they shot, they-- if we came in-- I had a [? outer ?] kommando, on [? outer ?] camp working-- and now and again as [?
SS ?] [? pass by, ?] they're not always there because there were kapos.
And you had to work.
You really had to work, you know?
We-- we-- we carried stones and throw on-- you know, they made a road or something.
And if they came by, they don't mind to hit you.
I remember once that one boy was run over by a horse, and horse bolted and hit a boy, and the boy fell.
And an SS come by, he run to that horse and left the boy bleeding there to death.
Never looked at him, but he looked at the horse.
That's how they were.
They were cruel.
They-- they-- Most of the SS there were not even Germans.
They were Ukrainian, or Croats, or Dutch, even, or all nationalities.
And one little example, and I-- after I got another kommando, in-- We worked on the [GERMAN]-- were in the street-- and we had to throw stones on trucks.
And they were manned by chauffeurs, and they called them NSKK.
I don't know if you've heard the name.
That is National Socialist and [INAUDIBLE].
They had a swastika and a round cap.
They were working behind the front, and they-- and they were always seen walking with a spade.
And one day-- and they changed all the time over-- a-- a kommando came.
There were about a dozen.
And one boy was very friendly-- tried to.
He immediately, the same day, gave cigarettes to the kapos and the-- you had kaps and you had civil arbiters, civilian from out of camp who were Polish, mainly.
There were some [?
Poland-- ?] [?
Polish, ?] but they were Nazis.
Germans, or Jews.
Kapos, yeah.
Inmates from camp, mainly Polish.
Polish kapos.
And he tried to be very friendly.
And the next day he started contacting with us, and we were frightened.
And he asked the kapo-- gave cigarettes again-- can I talk to us?
We had a group of about a dozen-- a dozen boys, and he started giving us some food and warning us to be careful for him, him, and him, but not him, him, and him.
There were quite a few Dutch amongst them.
Yeah, there were Dutch-- five or six Dutch amongst them, and Danish.
And-- and-- and-- not-- not German.
Five Dutch were there, and a few from Belgium and a few from Denmark, and that's it.
And he told me, they are good and they are no good.
And-- and after a day or two, he started asking names.
He said, my name is [?
Yapi, ?] and he knew a lot of Jews.
He said, I'm from the [INAUDIBLE], and my friends were Jews.
And do you know so-and-so?
And he asked only names and names and names and names and asking-- of we in camp all the time, asking-- if we-- if there's anyone there of that name.
But he knew a hell of a lot of Jews.
You know, too many.
And we never found anyone.
No.
And after the war, I was back in Amsterdam.
I saw him sitting on a terrace, and I went to him.
And I said, you know, [?
Yapi. ?] And he looked at me and said, who are you?
I said, my name is Sam.
I was in Monowitz.
He wouldn't believe.
He was standing up and started hugging me.
He said, can I talk to you?
I said yes.
He said, I'm Ruben Schwartz.
It was a Jewish boy.
He is the only one who was in the [? whole ?] of the line.
He was in Auschwitz-- in Monowitz is-- is an Aussenlager-- he was there, but he couldn't-- he had no contact with the outside world.
He-- I met him quite a few times after, because I was with my aunt.
She took him straightaway home.
I lost contact with him.
He's also an old man now, like me.
But amazing that a boy-- a Jewish boy-- he was protect-- he was circumcised, and he told, and the other boys protected him always, when he had to shower.
You know, because the-- but he came right through the war.
And in Monowitz, the cruelty.
The-- I had a friend working with me, and he was hated.
The boy was so strong that he was hated by the kapos because he was so strong.
He was-- and-- and-- you know, some boys look strong.
You know, he had muscles and arms, and they always really went against him.
And one day, we have to carry [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things from the barrack, from one to the other one, and that kapo-- a Polish kapo-- hit him, the friend of mine.
I call him friend because we shared everything.
He hit, and he lost his temper and gave him a-- a-- and gave him a smack in his face.
He was very strong.
Normally, if you hit a kapo, that's the end of it, but we had an SS man walking with us.
He said, oh, oh!
Oh.
Start fighting.
Sadist.
And they had to fight.
And it was also strong man, and that-- that friend of mine hit the man that much he finished up in-- in hospital.
I'd never seen him back.
I don't know if he died.
I've never seen him, anyway.
But they were so sadistic.
They'll make you-- if they had a chance, they'll make you fight to death, like in Rome in the-- in the-- and they enjoy it.
Oh, nothing.
He was just go back to-- he died.
One day, I lost him.
He went into another direction.
I've been told after, he died one day before the war-- before the end of the war.
Somewhere in-- in-- I don't know.
Very, very strong boy.
And that's what I liked.
I liked-- Always, if you were in camp too strong or too weak, that's no good.
You have to be in between, like me.
Not-- not-- never go in front and never go in back, always go in the center.
Never go right in front.
And-- and then-- I have to tell more stories-- then I-- in Monowitz, I could-- I could start play some music.
They gave me better kommando.
They made me a Maler, a painter.
I was camouflaging the-- you call it, the-- the barracks, painting the rooms from the-- from the Germans.
We were painting.
We had paint, and we painted-- like, I'm a good painter now, I still can do it.
I have learned the trade to be a painter, and that gives you a little bit lighter kommando and a little bit extra work, you know, extra food.
Sometimes an SS man threw a piece of bread to you, or, you know, they-- they-- they had fun with you.
They could sit there and eat, and-- and-- and we work there, and then the bones-- they threw a bone over and we were fighting for the bone.
They-- they're sadists.
They were sadists.
They-- they-- they-- You see, Monowitz was not in a-- a destroying lager, like Auschwitz.
It was in a-- a working lager, but everyone died there.
Six weeks, you die.
People don't even live there.
And the disease there-- there-- typhus, they-- they died from typhus by the-- the-- by the thousands.
I mean, the-- it was very lucky that I never-- never contracted it.
I don't know.
We all were loused and dirty and-- and filthy.
And-- and-- We had to-- once a week, we shower in the cold.
Below zero, in the nude, it's freezing cold, [INAUDIBLE] shower and out.
You couldn't survive it, those things.
I don't know.
I still can't-- the-- the story comes later on, at getting worse.
We don't know.
There's no explanation for it, how we-- how survived.
Don't know.
I'm not a hero.
I'm not a-- a-- a-- I-- I would say that everyone who came out of camp was lucky-- was just plain lucky.
If you don't get sick, if you were in the right time and the right place, you had the right kommando-- of course, you saw them going left and right around you.
I've seen in Monowitz already-- I was at Monowitz about six, seven months also.
I don't know times, exactly.
And you see them coming and going, come and go all the time.
They came.
Two, three, four, five weeks, they were gone.
Dead.
You know, you ask sometimes, where is-- oh, he's gone.
There's no feeling, either.
There's no feeling for each other.
It's just a matter of surviving.
Not bad.
Not-- not-- All right.
We were eating the whole day, talking about what my mother used to cook.
[LAUGHTER]
We were so hungry.
What was that?
What was your favorite meal?
It-- it-- we did cakes, and the chicken soups, and-- and what we ate, it was fantastic.
Looked like thirsty, the water runs out of your mouth.
[LAUGHTER]
Oh, definitely, yes.
Oh, definitely, because we had even a few non-Jewish Dutch in-- in Auschwitz.
They were from an underground, and they were also amongst us, yes.
Well, and then in Monowitz, unexpected, I was called.
And there were a few diamond cutters, and we were called up again, the few who were left.
Someone had an idea-- one of the Germans had an idea again.
We were called up.
They had us registered, and we went on transport to Auschwitz.
And I was in Auschwitz, in main lager.
And I kept me-- we found out later, they wanted to transport us to Bergen-Belsen.
They-- they want to build a diamond factory again.
We found out later, much later.
And we went to Auschwitz in a barrack, and it was block 11, the worst block.
Block 10 was the experimental block, and in between block 11 and 10, you've got the famous platz where everyone was killed there.
And-- and-- and-- you've heard about that block?
And we were sitting-- What did you-- what did you see of that block?
Oh, we were-- every day, we could look out of the window.
Shooting, the-- the-- the hitting.
Even SS were there in-- in-- you know SS-- were sitting there in jail.
It wasn't jail.
They-- they-- they hang there, it was terrible.
You could look out of the window-- screaming there, in that-- in that platz there.
But we were upstairs, and were sitting for several weeks.
They forgot about us.
No.
We were sitting there, upstairs, looking out of the other window.
Could see the whole-- in those days, it was the time of the big Hungarian transports.
That must've been, oh, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] June, July, August '44-- about that time.
And the-- the sky was red.
We knew-- then we knew-- then, we knew.
Red.
They came-- the transport came from-- in those days, and it went right through to the gas chambers.
Right through.
And I was in Auschwitz, and Birkenau is only six, seven kilometers-- I don't know the distance, six-- we walked there often.
Six, seven kilometers, about.
And suddenly, we were called on-- downstairs because we have to go with the kommandos.
Apparently the transport was over, and they asked me, what are you doing?
I said, I'm a Maler.
I'm a painter.
Where has the [INAUDIBLE]?
He said, Monowitz.
Maler.
In-- in-- in house, I was a painter.
And I got a kommando in painting.
They put me in an-- in an-- So while you were up there, you would just-- were you allowed to go out during the day?
No.
Oh, yes, we went out.
At night time, we could go to that-- that square and-- to have a little bit of air, but not much.
No, we were there the whole day, sitting there.
Not so many weeks-- three, four weeks maybe, I don't know.
Nothing.
Nothing doing.
Laying in bed.
It's good.
And-- not long, a few weeks.
And then I came in kommando, I became a Maler, a painter.
Music, no.
No chance.
Polish there were in command, and there was too many of them.
And-- and I tried to, no.
No chance.
I tried to get in, yes.
Well, too many of them.
Too many musicians.
Not enough-- not enough plan-- no room.
Waiting list.
[LAUGHS] No, no.
Come again, no.
No hope.
No hope in Auschwitz.
There were more musicians than instruments.
They need only, say, a dozen or two dozen, how it was.
And then if you play the violin-- and it was full, there was not enough, and then that's it.
Yeah, they-- the Polish, they-- they were first.
No, there was Polish kapos and then Polish musicians there.
The Hungarians, maybe.
I-- I never tried, anyway-- I had a good kommando.
All right.
You mean the Polish--
Yeah, and then we were already well established, you see.
We knew our way already.
We already old inmates.
And I was in camp already nearly two years.
And-- and I-- look, in the beginning, if you put some bread in your pocket and you sleep at night, the next day it is gone.
They cut it out of your pocket, or they steal it from you.
So we learned to eat everything that you've got immediately.
And-- and-- you see, in the-- in the barracks, you get a piece of bread every day-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]
You get a piece of bread every day, and a-- a piece of butter or what it is there, and eat it in one go.
And then you're got a plate of-- how you call it-- soup.
You know, you had a little plate you put all your food in.
I ate-- virtually, ate once a day.
Looked like it was water.
It was nothing.
It-- it--
I remember if we walked in camp-- and we walked out, we marched in-- and then we ask, [GERMAN]?
And the little one said, [GERMAN].
I never forget.
[INAUDIBLE] soup.
Heavy soup we've got today.
You know, we always asked, what are we eating?
And I must tell you a little story.
And so we walked in-- that was Auschwitz-- and I worked.
I was a painter.
And-- we re-camouflaged the roofs, and we painting the barracks, and then the SS, they wanted half the room with another color, and-- and then within reason, but they can't hit you when you paint.
It was a good kommando, you know?
To-- it was a prominent kommando in camp.
Beg pardon?
Who?
Maler kommando.
Malers.
Painters.
But his name?
No, paint kommando-- we were Malers.
We were Malers kommando.
Maler is painter.
And-- and we had those-- how you call it, paint-- how you call it when you have camouflage paint, spritz paint-- we-- we blew it on the roofs, that-- that-- how you call it-- canisters.

And-- and we parked our canisters near the-- the potatoes and the-- the-- and vegetables of camp, so we could pinch there what we want to, and eat it because it stand in the same home, more or less-- more or less.
But you can't eat it, it's raw.
You can't eat a raw potato.
So we were thinking, how can we get that-- that food in camp?
And the one had a very-- he was Hungarian, Hungarians are smart-- had a bright idea.
He had a bright idea.
He said, look.
We take that-- we take the canister, we take them in camp.
And we went to one of the SS-- they had one all the time there-- and we-- we always had a rank.
[GERMAN].
We always gave them a rank higher.
Hey, [GERMAN], this canister is kaput.
[GERMAN].
Can we-- can we not bring it in lager?
And we bring it back?
And he said, yeah, good.
[INAUDIBLE].
Name, first your name.
And so we would start carrying that canister, that very thing there, with a piece of rope, in camp.
And then as soon as we walked through the door-- stop.
What's your bloody thing doing there?
Yes, we have to bring it in lager because [GERMAN]-- or [? commader, ?] that his name was-- told us that we have to look after it, because it's always damaged every day.
It wasn't true.
And so we walked with that bloody thing for nearly the whole week, up and down and up, and they got used to it.
And then after, I would say, five or six days, we cleaned the thing out, hosed it out, and we put it full with potatoes and vegetables-- [LAUGHS] mainly potatoes-- and we carried it in camp.
Nobody looked at us.
And we emptied it in camp, and we had potatoes-- green potatoes and brown potatoes.
[LAUGHS] And we picked them in the-- we had fires, you know-- and we put them in.
And we divided amongst ourselves.
We were at one barrack, all the Malers in one-- in one.
Block six, I remember.
And we all had to get one, and you one potato, and you one.
And it helps a little bit.
A little bit, you know?
It was something.
Did they ever find out?
No.
Look, if they find out they kill you.
They never found out.
And we did it for quite awhile.
I mean, a long time.
We did it for-- for-- and we went on to-- oh, we worked quite-- I even worked in Birkenau.
You know, in-- in Birkenau in-- We were very good painters, with a good kommando, and we did quite a few works for the commandant.
Normal, you know, like a house.
We were in there, and--
You had to be very careful, you know?
Say there was one-- I remember this.
There was one house, and there was a little child, a boy.
And he spit one right in his face.
You can't wipe your face off.
I remember that.
No, no, no.
Someone else, in his face.
Right in his face.
Nothing.
Nothing.
Can do nothing.
You can't-- you can't even move.
You couldn't even-- even-- even look around, just look forward.
Not left, not right.
They shouldn't see you.
You-- you lived in-- how can I say?
Did we live in fear?
No.
Our attitude was hard.
You were not human anymore.
Sometimes you asked, who am I?
Am I an animal?
Who are-- who are we?
What are we?
What did we do?
You see all those people around you, all innocent people.
And-- and our-- our kapos were criminals, mm.
And they commanded us.
And then you start thinking sometimes, who are we?
What is there?
Where are we?
It's hard to explain.
Did you come up with any answers?
Beg your pardon?
No.
Oh yes, I came up with answers.
There's not much up there.
That's my answer.
The people ask me, why don't you?
He must have seen it.
The cruelty, he must have seen it.
And you see, even, I was in-- in men's lagers.
I had very little to do with women-- say, not at all.
How were those women treated?
Like in block 10?
Did you ever think about how-- what had happened to your wife and mother?
Yeah.
With that kind of feeling.
You know, after the war I never tried to find out everything, who was alive, because I knew.
I knew.
I mean, I was there.
I was on it, in it-- in it.
I was too long in Auschwitz to know.
I've been in the gas chambers.
I've been in the gas chambers.
I'll tell you that after.
I'll tell you.
Well, in-- I would say I was still painting up till-- I was still a Maler for quite a few months.
And it must have been December, [? '40. ?] We knew-- we knew what was going on in the world.
We know we had--
Was told.
Maybe from the SS to-- for about us.
We knew that the Allies were there.
We knew they landed in Europe.
We knew.
We knew the war was close by, and we knew the Russians were very close.
We knew that.
We didn't know how close, but we knew that.
And the gas-- the gassing was finished in-- they had also that big-- they blew one of the gas chambers up.
I was there.
I was in Auschwitz.
I was there.
But we-- we knew what had happened in Birkenau, but not exactly.
We knew there was a-- there was a sabotage going on, we knew that.
I was in Auschwitz.
You see, Auschwitz is--
--10, 15 kilometers away.
But the gas chambers were in Birkenau, then Auschwitz.
You know, they were two separate camps.
I was in main lager.
Birkenau, it was worse, there were small barracks.
I mean-- And one day, we went up and [? kommando, ?] and we marched to the gas chambers where there was nothing working anymore.
And they wanted us to hit holes, you know, with the pig and hammer-- holes in the ground.
They want to blow them up.
I don't think they ever blew them up.
All the evidence had to go, so we knew it was close.
Very strange.
Very strange.
I-- matter of fact, the gas chamber-- the-- the-- the oven-- next to the oven-- I remember.
I remember it well.
There was always ash.
I touched it.
It was part of a body, you know, burned, half-burned.
When you touch it, it fell apart.
We had no feeling.
You know, we-- we-- we went in the-- in the, how you call it, the gas chamber.
We went-- we went in-- in the gas chamber-- how you call it, like showers with little things hanging there.
And-- and-- and outside, it was beautiful green, beautiful green.
And-- and-- it looks, you know, when the people came in, they didn't know where they went to.
I never experienced it, you know, but that's how it was.
They kept it outside, very nice.
And how many gas chambers were there?
Three or four?
So when you went in, it was a really weird-- strange feeling in there?
Yeah.
Like our feeling was gone, but it's still a very strange feeling.
Two-- two days I went there.
Oh yes, we worked.
Yes, amongst ourselves, yeah.
Talked-- we always talked.
That they were dead.
We knew they were dead.
We knew.
We were alive.
They were dead.
We knew that.
But who was that?
We didn't know.
I know there was no one left.
I've seen people, and then they were gone.
It was-- it was no existence.
You were-- you were not a human, you know?
You were-- and then that went on in Auschwitz till [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 19 of January. '40--?
You could hear rumbling.
You could hear it, far away.
You know, zzz.
You know?
You know something was going on.
And unexpected, appell.
The whole camp, appellplatz, in Auschwitz.
Commandant Hoss-- Hess-- Hoss-- you heard the name?
The camp we be evacuated, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everyone who stays behind will be shot.
The camp will we blown up, and no one will come out alive.
It's up to yourself.
You could hide, easy.
I know people that hid, and they came out of it.
And we were the thousands in appell.
Back to the barrack.
There's nothing there.
They start marching.
Only German, oh yes.
Well, Dutch and German, there's not a great deal of difference.
You picked up your words.
I could speak some German.
I could speak German, if I'd learned in school.
And-- and-- I could speak good English.
I mean, you know, it makes a little bit-- we were quite well educated over there, Holland.
It makes a difference.
